coredata.dc:title: Preparation of oil-soluble carbon-dot based room temperature phosphorescent materials by surface modification
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Huang, Xinyu

Liu, Yingliang
Liu, Xiaotang
coredata.dc:description: The strategy of preparing carbon dots-based room temperature phosphorescent materials by using inorganic substrate to promote the triplet excited transition of carbon dots (CDs) has been extensively studied. The stable existence of room temperature phosphorescent materials prepared by inorganic substrate materials in the organic phase is still a great challenge. In this study, a new strategy for obtaining oil-soluble CDs@SiO2 room temperature phosphorescent materials by surface modification were proposed. The hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chain was introduced on the surface of CDs@SiO2 composites by esterification reaction to obtain oil-soluble CDs@SiO2 room temperature phosphorescent materials (SSA composites). The decay lifetime of SSA composites is 1.263 s, SSA composites phosphors have a quantum yield (QY) of 48 %. Finally, the application of SSA composites in electrostatic spinning successfully improves the hydrophobicity of nano spun films and expands the new function of electrostatic spinning in anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescent materials
Carbon dots
Silicon dioxide
Oil-soluble
Surface modification
Electrostatic spinning

Structure and morphology of SSA composites
Application of SSA composites in electrospinning

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation mechanism and technical roadmap of SSA composites.
(a-b) HRTEM of CDs, (c) particle size of CDs, (d-f) HRSEM of SSA composites.

(a) FT-IR of CDs, SiO2, CDs@SiO2 composites, and SSA composites, (b) XRD of CDs, SiO2, CDs@SiO2 composites, and SSA composites, (c-e) TGA and DTG analysis of SiO2, CDs@SiO2 composites, and SSA composites, (f-i) The XPS characterization of SSA composites is total spectrum, Si spectrum, respectively, Oxygen spectrum, carbon spectrum.

(a) Normalized UV–vis absorption, fluorescence absorption, phosphorescence absorption of SSA composites, (b) Full band FL emission of SSA composites, (c) Full-band PL emission of SSA composites, (d) Phosphorescent lifetime decay for SSA composites, (e) Phosphorescent lifetime decay for CDs@SiO2 composites, (f) Liquid afterglow map of SSA composites.

(a) Phosphorescence emission 3D plots of SSA composites under different pH environments, (b) Phosphorescence emission 3D plots of SSA composites under different oil-phase environments, (c) Afterglow plots of SSA composites under different pH environments, (d) Afterglow plots of SSA composites under different oil-phase environments, (e) Daylight of SSA composites under different pH environments, (f) Daylight of SSA composites under different oil-phase environments.
Luminescence mechanism diagram of SSA composites.
(a) SEM of blank nanofilm, (b) contact angle of blank nanofilm, (c) SEM of SSA nanofilm, (d) contact angle of SSA nanofilm.
(a-c) Anti-counterfeiting characterization of blank nanofilms, (d-e) anti-counterfeiting properties of SSA nanofilms.

head.ce:title: Preparation of oil-soluble carbon-dot based room temperature phosphorescent materials by surface modification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biomass Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biomass Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biomass Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The strategy of preparing carbon dots-based room temperature phosphorescent materials by using inorganic substrate to promote the triplet excited transition of carbon dots (CDs) has been extensively studied. The stable existence of room temperature phosphorescent materials prepared by inorganic substrate materials in the organic phase is still a great challenge. In this study, a new strategy for obtaining oil-soluble CDs@SiO2 room temperature phosphorescent materials by surface modification were proposed. The hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chain was introduced on the surface of CDs@SiO2 composites by esterification reaction to obtain oil-soluble CDs@SiO2 room temperature phosphorescent materials (SSA composites). The decay lifetime of SSA composites is 1.263 s, SSA composites phosphors have a quantum yield (QY) of 48 %. Finally, the application of SSA composites in electrostatic spinning successfully improves the hydrophobicity of nano spun films and expands the new function of electrostatic spinning in anti-counterfeiting.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fig. 1.Surface modification of CD@SiO2 composites using oleic acid and utilization to electrostatic spinning

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, CDs have been widely used in many fields such as biological imaging [1], information anti-counterfeiting encryption [2], optoelectronics [3], due to their excellent photoluminescence properties, simple preparation, safety, low toxicity, strong chemical stability. However, owing to the influence of triplet exciton non-radiative transition [4], it is difficult for CDs to exhibit stable phosphorescence[5]. In 2013, Deng et al. research group first dispersed CDs in polyvinyl alcohol PVA medium to prepare CDs-based room temperature phosphorescent materials with a decay life of 338 ms [6].

Since then, with the in-depth study of CDs-based room temperature phosphorescent materials, it is found that the construction of CDs-based room temperature phosphorescent materials needs to meet the following conditions. 1. High intersystem cross (ISC) efficiency [7]. 2. Nonradiative relaxation of low CDs [8]. In view of the above problems, a solution is proposed. 1. Introducing heavy atoms such as halogen [9], heteroatoms such as nitrogen [10], phosphorus [11], to promote the spin-orbit coupling of electrons. 2. Introducing rigid matrix [12]. The non-radiative relaxation pathway [13], CDs is inhibited by matrix network or hydrogen bond [14], covalent bond crosslinking [15].

Based on the above discussion, researchers have developed a variety of CDs-based room temperature phosphorescent materials with different substrates and optical properties. According to different rigid matrices, room temperature phosphorescent materials are divided into organic matrix and inorganic matrix. The matrix of organic-room temperature phosphorescent materials mainly includes urea [16], biuret [17], cyanuric acid (CA) [18], polyurethane [19], polyvinyl alcohol [6], and so on. The matrix of inorganic-room temperature phosphorescent materials mainly includes boric acid [20,21], SiO2 [22,23] , zeolite [24], aluminum sulfate [25], and potassium bauxite [26]. Each has its own strengths and weaknesses. Organic materials, as the matrix for preparing room temperature phosphorescent materials, have the advantages of flexible structural tunability [27]. However, most organic-room temperature phosphorescent materials still have defects such as low luminous efficiency. Using inorganic materials as the host of room temperature phosphorescence boasts long life [28,29], high luminous efficiency [23], but the disadvantage is that it is unstable in an organic environment.

Although many matrix materials can be used with CDs to construct afterglow materials and have unique advantages in terms of performance and applications. However, optical materials constructed from organic substrates are not only costly, but also toxic and unstable in their optical properties. In practical research and applications, it is more important to construct green, highly stable and high quantum yield optical materials. In this process, we found that inorganic matrix room-temperature phosphorescent materials show immeasurable advantages and value in practical applications.

Our group has carried out some research on the optical properties of materials. In 2019, Li et al. prepared CDs silicon-based room temperature phosphorescent materials with ultra-long afterglow in an aqueous environment using silicon dioxide as a rigid matrix, and for the first time proposed the application in the actual imaging of biological samples [1]. In 2020, Sun et al. constructed a rigid Si-O-Si network using a silica matrix, which was encapsulated by biomass carbon polymer dots prepared from rice husk. Ultra-long room temperature phosphorescent materials with afterglow phenomenon greater than 40 s (naked eye) were prepared [30]. In 2020, He prepared CDs@SiO2 materials by sol-gel method, and prepared afterglow materials with very stable optical properties, which have afterglow phenomenon in both solid and liquid [31]. In 2021, using SiO2 as an inorganic matrix, MO et al. introduced heavy atom fluorine F by fluorine-nitrogen co-doping, and constructed afterglow materials excited by visible light [32]. These studies have amply demonstrate that the utilization of silica as a substrate can improve the optical properties of carbon-dot-based inorganic room-temperature phosphorescent materials and broaden their application directions.

Most inorganic room temperature phosphorescent materials have high quantum yield and ultra-long afterglow properties, but it is still challenging to maintain stable optical properties in the oil phase environment. Improving the optical stability of inorganic materials has become an urgent problem in practical applications, especially in the complex oil phase environment. The special chemical composition and physical properties of oil-phase environments often adversely affect the luminescence performance of inorganic composites, leading to the reduction of luminescence efficiency, shortening of afterglow time, or even complete extinction, thus limiting the widespread popularization of carbon-dot-based inorganic composites in practical applications.

At present, it is very common to improve the properties of materials by surface modification, so as to obtain ideal materials. Surface modification plays an indispensable role in improving the mechanical stability [33,34], thermal stability [35], and waterproof coating [36] of the composites. Among surface modification materials, SiO2 is widely used as a modified substrate to obtain the target material because of its safety, non-toxicity and stable chemical properties. Wang et al., used the surfactant polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) as a modifier to modify the SiO2 nanoparticles and incorporated them into the polymer to improve the mechanical properties of the composites [37]. In 2023, Han et al. added SiO2 synthesized by sol-gel method to the oil absorption skeleton, by adjusting the size of the pores to improve the oil absorption performance of the material, reduce the oil content in the wastewater, and reduce the water pollution [38]. In summary, the research on improving the properties of composite materials using surface modification has been very intensive, but the research on improving the optical properties of materials is still in its infancy. At present, carbon dot-based phosphorescent materials prepared on inorganic substrates are outstanding in terms of optical properties such as quantum yield and ultra-long afterglow characteristics. How to improve the optical stability of inorganic phosphorescent materials in oil phase environment is still a great challenge.

In order to achieve the desired results, we have devised a simple, convenient and environmentally friendly method. In this study, utilizes oleic acid as a surfactant for surface modification of silica composites based on carbon dots. Through the synergistic effect of non-covalent and covalent modifications, we successfully obtained an oil-soluble inorganic room-temperature phosphorescent material. Most importantly, this material is able to maintain stable phosphorescence in oil-soluble environments and different acid-base conditions, which fully validating the stability of the phosphorescent properties of our SSA composites. In addition, we have combined SSA composites with electrostatic spinning technology to make SSA nanohydrophobic films with dual anti-counterfeiting features. This innovation greatly enhances the hydrophobicity of the original nanofilm and provides new research ideas for currency anti-counterfeiting and artwork preservation.

All chemical reagents were unpurified and ready to use upon receipt, all reagents used in this experiment were analytically pure and Oleic acid (OA) was obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Ammonia (25 %-28 %), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOs, >99 %), Polyvinyl Butyral Resin (PVB, AR), Polyacrylic acid (PAA, AR) and Absolute ethanol (>99.7 %) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Reagent Co., Ltd., n-hexane (97 %) was purchased from Run Jie Chemical Co. Ltd., Ethylenediamine (EDA, AR) was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (China). At the same time, the deionized water required for this experiment was provided by the nano-pure water purifier system (Master-E, high-tech scientific tool, Shanghai, China).

Firstly, the photoluminescence properties and afterglow properties of SSA composites were characterized by spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi). In order to obtain its phosphorescence life, the decay life of the composite material under the phosphorescence phenomenon was obtained by the spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi).

In order to clearly show the characteristics and morphology of the composite sample, the sample was observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Talos F200S HRTEM FEI) at 200 kV acceleration voltage. The shape of the SSA composites sample was observed by high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (Verios HRSEM FEI), and the growth state of the SSA composites sample was amplified to nanometers. Transmission electron microscopy TEM (Talos L120C) was used to obtain a complete picture of the micron-sized sample.

Using KBr as the matrix, the characteristic spectra of different groups vibration from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 were obtained by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet IS10 Thermo Fisher). The infrared spectra of CDs, SiO2, CDs@SiO2 composites, SSA composites were obtained, compared and analyzed.

In order to further verify the success of the modification, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy XPS (Thermo Fisher Nexsa) was used to analyze the chemical state of the surface of the composite material, and all binding energies were corrected according to 284.5 eV of C1s.

Through the X-ray diffraction analyzer (XRD) was used to scan in the range of 10 ° - 80 ° at a speed of 10 °/min. Thermal analysis was performed using a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG209 F1Libra TM) at a reaction environment of 20 ° /min and 900 °C under nitrogen ventilation. In addition, the modification of the group surface can be judged according to the change of the sample mass before and after thermogravimetric analysis. The prepared electrostatically spun film was tested using a contact angle meter (JC2000 C1) to observe its hydrophobicity.

0.45 g of PAA and 840 μL of EDA were added to 30 mL of deionized water in turn, and ultra sounded for 30 min to make it fully dispersed. The mixed solution is transferred to a 50 mL PTFE-lined high-pressure reactor. The reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction is completed, the reactor is cooled to room temperature naturally, and then the reactant is taken out. Firstly, a 0.22 μm filter membrane was used to remove agglomerated large particles and reactive impurities. Ultrapure water was used as the dialysis solvent, and the initially filtered solution was placed in a dialysis bag (500 Da) for magnetic stirring for 2 h to further remove the unreacted small molecules. Finally, the obtained PAA-CDs solution was freeze-dried, and the solid powder was stored for subsequent use.

20 mg PAA-CDs powder was weighed and added to 10 mL deionized water, and the powder was completely dispersed in the solution by stirring. Then add TEOs 5 mL, ethanol 5 mL, ammonia 2 mL. It was transferred to a 150 mL flask and refluxed at 60 °C for 10 h. The obtained liquid samples were freeze-dried.

Take 5 mL of TEOs, 5 mL of ethanol, 2 mL of ammonia water, and 10 mL of deionized water and add them into a 150 mL beaker. Mix them thoroughly by ultrasonic oscillation. Then transfer the mixture into a 150 mL flask and reflux it at 60 °C for 10 h. Using the same reaction conditions, we prepare pure SiO2 material by simply removing the PAA-CDs powder. This pure SiO2 material will serve as a blank control for SSA composites in subsequent characterizations, facilitating analysis and comparison.

The mixture of oleic acid and n-hexane was obtained by adding 2 mL oleic acid to 30 mL n-hexane and heating at 60 °C for 5 min. Then 1.0 g CDs@SiO2 composites was added, and the mixture was stirred violently at 60 °C for 30 min. The mixture was washed several times with ethanol-water mixture, and the step was repeated more than three times. The sample was freeze-dried to obtain the SSA composites material.

A mixed solution of 10 wt% PVB and anhydrous ethanol was prepared at a mass ratio of 1:1. The 2 g SSA composites material was added into the mixed solution, and the mixed solution was stirred to obtain the electrospinning precursor solution. Then, SSA nano-electrospinning membrane was prepared by electrospinning device. The corresponding setting parameters are: applied voltage 15 kV, syringe boost speed 0.5 mL/min, drum speed 140 rpm, receiving distance 20 cm, relative temperature 23∼ 25 °C, relative humidity 30∼40 %. The prepared SSA nanofilm was dried in vacuum at 60 °C and sealed. In addition, under the same conditions, blank nanofilms not mixed with SSA composites were prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research strategies for designing and synthesizing materials
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the current research base on CDs@SiO2 composites. In this study, the strategy of preparing inorganic room-temperature phosphorescent materials first and then modifying them on their basis was adopted. As is shown in Fig. 1, firstly, water-soluble PAA-CDs were simply synthesized by hydrothermal method using PAA as carbon source, and EDA as nitrogen source. PAA-CDs contain a large number of polymer chains and functional groups on the surface, which help promote phosphorescence. In the heated state, TEOs is fully hydrolyzed to form silica [39]. Meanwhile, in this process, the silica generated in situ can completely wrap the CDs, which plays a role in limiting the vibrational jump of the CDs and reducing the non-radiative relaxation, thus stabilizing the phosphorescence emission.

In order to maintain the dispersion and stability of the material in a lipophilic environment, this study utilized oleic acid for surface modification of silica. Oleic acid, as an anionic surfactant, thus enhancing the dispersion of SiO2 in an oleophilic environment. Moreover, oleic acid can not only be used as a surfactant, but also has both hydrophilic carboxyl groups and lipophilic hydrophobic long chain hydrocarbon structure. This flexible long carbon chain structure provides great convenience for our modification. According to the sequence of the reaction, weak forces such as intermolecular van der Waals forces or electrostatic forces promoted oleic acid to form an adsorption layer on the surface of CDs@SiO2 composites quickly first. The carboxyl group -COOH at the hydrophilic end of the oleic acid and the hydroxyl group -OH on the surface of SiO2 then slowly underwent esterification. With the help of covalent bond condensation reaction, stable and strong ester groups were formed, and hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chains were introduced on the surface of CDs@SiO2 composites.

In summary, the modification measures mainly based on covalent binding and supplemented by non-covalent binding were adopted. Certain reaction conditions were used to promote the combination of CDs@SiO2 composites and hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chains, thus completing the surface modification. The hydrophobic hydrocarbon chains were tightly arranged on the surface to form a hydrophobic layer. Then with the help of various post-treatments to promote its dehydration and condensation, the final sample SSA composites were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of SSA composites
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the morphology and structural characteristics of the material, the SSA composites material would be analyzed by combining various characterizations. The morphology and size of the composites were investigated by various electron microscopes.

According to Fig. 2 (a-c), the morphology of CDs was observed under high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). the lattice fringes of the CDs are clearly shown to be 0.21 nm. It is consistent with the lattice spacing of graphite (100) [40]. The average particle size of CDs is about 4.5 nm. As is shown in Fig. 2 (d-f), the morphology of SSA composites at different magnifications was obtained. With the increase of SEM magnification, the SSA composites structure network was observed in depth. It is found that the overall uniform flakes are present at 2 μm. The dense arrangement of silica particles was observed under HRSEM. After analysis, it may be due to the introduction of hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chains, which enhanced the tightness of CDs@SiO2 materials. Combined with the results of HRTEM and SEM data, this study did not observe a regular structure of SiO2, which is mostly a spherical amorphous structure. Combined with the XRD characterization, it is consistently shown that the amorphous structure of SiO2 in the present composites [22].

In order to clarify the composition of SiO2, CDs@SiO2 composites and SSA composites, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy FT-IR, this study used X-ray diffraction XRD and thermogravimetric analysis TGA for preliminary analysis.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, through meticulous comparative studies, we were surprised to find that SiO2, SSA composites and CDs@SiO2 composites presented several common characteristic absorption peaks on the infrared spectrograms, which not only provided strong evidence for the material's matrix identification, but also revealed the potential chemical connections between them.

Specifically, the characteristic peaks that are common to SiO₂, SSA composites, and CDs@SiO₂ composites include the -OH stretching vibration peak at approximately 3450 cm⁻¹, the presence of which indicates the existence of silica hydroxyl groups in the material and serves as a characteristic signature of water molecules or other hydroxyl-containing functional groups. The Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching vibration peak at around 1110 cm⁻¹ and the Si-O-Si symmetric stretching vibration peak near 790 cm⁻¹, together, corroborate the solid existence of the silica skeleton in the material, which is a direct manifestation of the integrity of the Si-O-Si bonding network. Furthermore, the Si-O bending vibration peak observed at approximately 470 cm⁻¹ reinforces the stable structure of the SiO₂ matrix [22].

These common and significant characteristic absorption peaks not only conclusively verified the robustness of the Si-O-Si bonding network during the synthesis of the composites, but also clearly revealed that the overall structure of the materials remained intact without any significant changes even after the fine encapsulation of the CDs and subsequent surface modification treatments. This finding is crucial for understanding the chemical bonding interactions as well as the structural stability within the composites. Further, when we focus on the specificity analysis of the SSA composites, a phenomenon emerges that the material exhibits a clear and sharp absorption peak at a wave number position of about 2800 cm-¹. Through detailed spectral analysis and literature comparison, we confirm that this characteristic peak is attributed to the stretching vibration of the methylene CH₂ in the oleic acid molecule [41]. A prominent peak was distinctly observed in the vicinity of 1700 cm⁻¹, leading this study to speculate that it could be attributed to the stretching vibration of the carbonyl group within the ester moiety (-COO-), which is formed through the dehydration condensation reaction during esterification [42]. In summary, based on the two characteristic absorption peaks, it can be basically concluded that the esterification reaction did occur in the SSA composites. Therefore, based on the comprehensive FT-IR spectroscopy analysis, we can conclude that the surface of CDs@SiO2 composites successfully underwent esterification reaction and grafting of hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chains.

The structure of the sample was examined by XRD. As shown in Fig. 3 b, there is amorphous silica at 23 ° [22]. In Fig. 3 b, all three show broad peaks with no distinct peak phases. comparing the three curves, it is found that the morphology of SiO2, CDs@SiO2 composites and SSA composites are basically the same. Once again, it is verified that SiO2 as an intermediate medium is very stable in the construction of room temperature phosphorescent materials based on CDs and in the grafting of oil-soluble materials for modification.

The three sets of thermogravimetric profiles are SSA composites, simple silica and CDs@SiO2 composites, as shown in Fig. 3 (c-e). Through thermogravimetric analysis, we can be more clearly and explicitly informed of the changes in the mass of the material caused by temperature variations, and subsequently analyze its composition. During the material preparation stage, we deliberately prepare pure silica and CDs@SiO₂ composites in order to utilize the differences in their thermal stability for comparison with SSA composites. Consequently, we can indirectly ascertain the effectiveness of the surface modification.

Fig. 3 c exhaustively reveals the intricate pyrolysis journey of SSA composites, a process that starkly contrasts with the pyrolysis behaviors exhibited by pure SiO₂ and CDs@SiO₂ composites, showcasing a distinct two-stage decomposition pattern. Across a broad temperature range spanning from 0 °C to 900 °C, the SSA material undergoes a substantial total mass loss of 48.13 %, a value that surpasses that of CDs@SiO₂ composites by nearly 30 percentage points. A more profound examination of the TGA curves presented in Fig. 3 c disentangles the pyrolysis process of SSA composites into two distinct stages. In the initial stage, as the temperature ascends from 0 °C to 300 °C, the total mass diminishes by 29.42 %. Notably, a precipitous decline in mass, signified by a steep downward slope in the TGA curve, occurs proximate to 239.5 °C, underscoring a significant mass loss at this juncture. Drawing from the salient features of the TGA curves, we postulate that the primary during this low temperature interval (0 °C to 200 °C) involve the evaporation of free water and small organic molecules, such as residual solvents like ethanol. As the temperature escalates further into the 200 °C to 300 °C range, the observed mass loss is likely attributable to the rupture of ester bonds inherent within the composite structure, these bonds often stemming from the integration of oleic acid or its derivatives during the surface modification procedure. Transitioning to the second stage, where temperatures soar from 300 °C to 500 °C, the SSA composites undergo another pronounced mass loss. The mass variation during this phase is particularly pronounced, peaking at 440.1 °C, marking the apex of the pyrolysis process. It is plausible to deduce that at these elevated temperatures, the hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chains, specifically those affiliated with the methylene and methyl moieties of oleic acid, commence breaking down and volatilizing on a grand scale. The thermal lability of these extensive hydrocarbon structures stands as the primary reason behind the steep mass loss witnessed during this stage.

As shown in Fig. 3 d, pure silica consistently exhibits a characteristic two-step pyrolysis profile during thermogravimetric analysis, which occurs primarily in the temperature range from 90 °C to 450 °C. Specifically, at lower temperatures (0 °C∼ 52 °C), the abrupt change in the DTG curve coincides with the evaporation of free water remaining on the surface and in the pores of the material. This process is the dominant mode of mass loss during the initial pyrolysis phase. With the gradual increase in temperature, the DTG curve shows another significant temperature change near 244.4 °C, indicating that pyrolysis of the hydroxyl groups in the silica structure begins to dominate. At high temperatures, the hydroxyl groups (e.g., silanol groups) on the surface of the silica fracture, releasing water while leading to further mass loss. This process fully explains the structural properties of the silica matrix.

As shown in Fig. 3 e, the thermogravimetric analysis curves of the CDs@SiO₂ composites clearly outline three primary stages of mass variation。During the initial pyrolysis stage, which encompasses a gradual temperature rise from 0 °C to approximately 300 °C, the CDs@SiO₂ composites experiences a mass loss of 2.56 %. This mass decrease is primarily ascribed to the evaporation of small organic molecules (including residual solvents like ethanol from the preparation process) and free water present within the composites. As the temperature further climbs, the CDs@SiO₂ composites undergoes another notable mass loss of 4.9 % at around 193.4 °C. This mass loss phase is primarily attributed to the initiation of hydroxyl group fracture on the surface of the CDs@SiO₂ composites. Hydroxyl groups, which are ubiquitous on the surfaces of silica and carbon dots, exhibit relatively low thermal stability and tend to fracture at high temperatures, releasing water in the process. The culmination of the pyrolysis process is marked by a distinct mass loss peak at 565 °C. At this stage, the mass of the composite decreases by 2.69 %. We hypothesize that this mass reduction is primarily due to the rupture of the covalent bond between the CDs@SiO₂ composites. Under extreme temperatures, the covalent bond breaks down, resulting in the detachment of CDs from the SiO₂ matrix and a substantial decrease in the overall mass of the composites.

In order to further verify the degree of silica surface modification and product properties, this study adopted the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique to thoroughly analyze and elucidate the surface elemental composition of SSA composites. Through the XPS spectra presented in Fig. 3 (f-i), we can clearly observe that SSA composites are mainly composed of three elements: oxygen (O), carbon (C), and silicon (Si). In Fig. 3 g, we find that the peak of Si2p appears at 103.5 eV. Further subdivision reveals characteristic peaks of SiO2 and Si-O bonding at 103.3 eV and 103.7 eV, respectively. After fitting the Si spectra, the results uncover the presence of high binding energy within the SSA composites. In this study, it is inferred that this binding energy is attributed to SiO2, implying that the surface modification using carboxyl groups did not undermine the basic Si-O-Si skeleton of the SSA composites. In Fig. 3 h, the peak of the oxygen element O is pinpointed at 532.5 eV. Upon close examination, distinct peaks emerge at 532.0 eV, 532.5 eV, and 533.5 eV, corresponding to the Si-O bonding, Si-O-Si bonding, and O-H bonding, respectively. Likewise, from the O spectrum, we can clearly discern its high binding energy segment aligning with the Si-O-Si bond. Fig. 3 i, the prominent peak of C, is situated at 285 eV. Following fitting procedures, the spectrum showcases three peaks positioned at 284.5 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.5 eV. Through meticulous comparison and scrutiny, we surmise that these peaks presumably represent the C-H bond in the hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chain, the C-O bond in the ester group, and the CO bond, respectively. It's worth noting that the segment with the higher binding energy is ascribed to the C-H bond, bolstering our earlier hypothesis that this segment predominantly stems from the stable CH2 group in the hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chain. Incorporating the aforementioned comprehensive analysis of XPS data and experimental findings, we deduce that the individual meticulous single spectra not solely corroborate each other but also interdepend to forge a seamless evidentiary chain. This set of data amply demonstrates that SSA composites rooted in a silane network skeleton can also hold phosphorescence stably after the introduction of oleic acid and grafted methylene long hydrocarbon chains.

In order to explore the origin of room temperature phosphorescence of SSA composites, a variety of optical properties were characterized. In Fig. 4 a, the three curves from left to right are the normalized ultraviolet absorption spectrum, the phosphorescence absorption spectrum and the fluorescence absorption spectrum. The UV mapping shows that the present composite has UV absorption peaks near 280 nm and 345 nm, which correspond to the π-π* leap of CC and n→π* leap of CO respectively [9]. From the Fig. 4 a, it can be seen that the positions of the UV absorption spectra of SSA composites are very similar to those of the phosphorescence absorption spectra. Combined with the research on the source of phosphorescence in recent years, it is analyzed that it is most likely to be caused by the π-π* leap of CC and the n→π* leap of CO, which contribute greatly to the generation of phosphorescence in SSA composites [9].

The optical properties of room temperature phosphorescence of SSA composites were further characterized. The full band fluorescence excitation profile and full band phosphorescence excitation profile are shown in Fig. 4 (b-c) respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased from 240 nm to 400 nm band, and was strongest at 371 nm, and then began to weaken.

As shown in Fig. 4 c, from 220 nm to 370 nm, the peak of phosphorescence intensity is at 361 nm. In summary, the optimum excitation wavelength for phosphorescence of SSA composites was determined to be 361 nm and the optimum emission wavelength was 500 nm. The optimum excitation wavelength for fluorescence of SSA composites was 371 nm and the optimum emission wavelength was 465 nm.

It can be clearly seen that the fluorescence of SSA composites has a certain excitation dependence, while the phosphorescence has no obvious excitation dependence. It is well known that this is due to the presence of a large number of functional groups on the CD surface, each of which has its own optimal excitation and emission wavelengths. Therefore, it leads to the emergence of excitation dependence under fluorescence excitation at different wavelengths. Whereas phosphorescence is dominated by functional groups such as CO/CC, therefore the variation of the excitation wavelength only has the greatest effect on the intensity of phosphorescence. Therefore, the excitation dependence of SSA composites is not significant [43,44].

For SSA composites, the characterization of quantum yields and decay lifetimes greatly reflects their optical properties. At 361 nm excitation, the quantum yield of SSA composites is as high as 48 %. As shown in Fig. 4 d, we measured the phosphorescence decay lifetime of SSA composites and fitted it according to the triple exponential equation. We can calculate its average phosphorescence lifetime using the following equation:
τ
avg =
∑
α
i
τ
i
2
/
∑
α
i
τ
i
where τ
i is the decay time, αi is the corresponding finger forward factor of τ
i
. The average decay life of the SSA composites is obtained as shown in Fig. 4 d as 1.263 s. In order to verify the changes in the optical properties of the materials before and after modification, we also measured the phosphorescence decay lifetime of unmodified CDs@SiO2 composites at an excitation wavelength of 361 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 e, CDs@SiO2 composites decay lifetime is only 0.227 s. In contrast, the difference in decay life before and after modification is very large. After discussion and analysis, we believe that this is most likely due to the fact that the dense hydrophobic groups on the surface of the modified material protect the composites, thus weakening the attenuating effects caused by factors such as oxygen and water in the environment.
As shown in Fig. 4 f, when the 365 nm UV lamp is extinguished, SSA composites show an extremely long afterglow that is visible to the naked eye and lasts for more than 9 s.

As is shown in Fig. 5 (a, c, e), in this study, SSA composites were subjected to a series of solutions with different pH values in order to systematically evaluate their phosphorescence stability under various acid-base conditions. Through detailed 3D phosphorescence spectroscopy analysis, we found that the SSA material can effectively maintain its phosphorescence emission in solutions with different pH, a result that verifies the excellent stability of the phosphorescence properties of SSA composites. It is worth noting that the reaction between strong alkali and silica usually occurs, which can lead to the luminescence burst of phosphorescent materials, but the SSA composites can still exhibit strong phosphorescence emission under the extreme strong alkaline environment (pH=13). This fully indicates that the hydrophobic oil film layer accessed after modification effectively protects the CDs@SiO2 composites and sufficiently isolates the silica from the direct contact with strong alkali, thus attenuating the extent of the luminescence burst phenomenon.

In order to explore the stability of phosphorescence properties after surface modification of CDs@SiO2 composites. The SSA composites were dispersed in different acid-base environments (pH level=1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13) and oil-soluble environments with different polarities.

According to Fig. 5 (b, d, f), SSA materials exhibit obvious afterglow phenomena in diverse polar environments. Furthermore, they can be stably dispersed in oil-soluble environments. It is well-known that conventional inorganic room temperature phosphorescent materials often agglomerate in oily media. However, through modification, it becomes evident that SSA composites improve this phenomenon. The introduction of hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chains effectively isolates the CDs@SiO2 composites within the SSA composites from direct contact with oily solvent molecules, achieving this through a physical barrier effect.

In order to investigate the reason for the room temperature phosphorescence produced by the present material, the possible luminescence mechanism was investigated in conjunction with the optical characteristics of the samples. As is shown in Fig. 6 , the reasons for phosphorescence emission from SSA composites are divided into three main layers. Firstly, under UV irradiation jumps from the ground state (S0) to the singlet state (Sn) via absorption of excitation light. Then, it undergoes internal conversion (IC) to the lowest singlet state (S1), at which time most of the electrons return directly to the ground state (S0) by radiative excitation, and fluorescence emission is generated at this time. Then, under the action of spin-orbit coupling, there are a small number of electrons through the intersystem scrambling (ISC) to the triplet state (T0 or T1), and then return to the ground state (S0) from the triplet state, and then there is a phosphorescence emission [45].

It is worth emphasizing that the introduction of hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chains plays a crucial role in enhancing the phosphorescence performance of SSA composites. These long-chain hydrocarbon molecules are tightly wrapped around the carbon dot-based silica composites, effectively limiting the vibrational and rotational freedom of the luminescent center molecules through both physical and chemical effects, thus greatly suppressing the non-radiative leap pathway.

The use of functional groups on the surface of carbon dots as luminescent centers to construct room-temperature phosphorescent materials has been widely studied (Fig. S1). In the optical property study of SSA composites, the n → π* transition of CO on the PAA-CDs surface acts as the luminescent center and limits its space of motion using a rigid matrix silica. The role of the rigid matrix is to reduce the vibrational rotation of the molecules within it and decrease the non-radiative relaxation. This increases the number of triple excitons returning to the ground state, ultimately increasing the phosphorescence intensity. The hydrophobic long hydrocarbon chains not only act as oil-soluble providers, but also play a role as the outermost layer of the medium, further protecting the phosphorescent body.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of SSA composites in electrospinning
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the stable room temperature phosphorescence properties and unique oil solubility of SSA composites, this study combined it with electrospinning for preliminary exploration of applications. The purpose of the study is to prepare a nano-scale hydrophobic film with encryption and anti-counterfeiting function by combining the advantages of the two. So as to provide new research ideas for the preservation of high-level art and luxury goods, as well as the anti-counterfeiting and encryption of paper currency.

As is shown in the Fig. 7 (a, c), the blank nanofiber membrane and the nanofiber membrane doped with SSA composites ligand are observed by scanning electron microscopy and contact angle. Under the scanning electron microscope (SEM), it was observed that the blank film only contained disordered nanofiber scaffolds. In the nanofiber membrane doped with SSA composites material, it can be clearly seen that there is a layer of uniform film on the surface of the original nanofiber frame. The reason for this phenomenon is most likely that the doped SSA composites are uniformly distributed on the nanofiber skeleton by virtue of their excellent oil solubility, which fills the network voids between the original fiber structures.

Through the contact angle test, it was clearly observed that the size of the infiltration angle before and after doping had a significant change Fig. 7 (b, d). The contact angle of the blank nanofilm was 79.28 °, and the infiltration angle of the SSA nanofilm was 97.59 °. The increase of the wetting angle also represents the improvement of the hydrophobicity of the nanofilm after the incorporation of the SSA composites materials.

In order to deeply investigate and verify the specific effects on the optical properties of nanofiber membranes before and after the addition of SSA composites, we conducted a comprehensive comparison and analysis. We found that different doping amounts of oleic acid led to changes in the phosphorescence properties and hydrophobicity of SSA composites. Through the changes of contact angle and phosphorescence intensity, we concluded that the optical properties and hydrophobicity of SSA composites have better compatibility when the oleic acid doping amount is 0. 794 wt% (Fig. S1).

As shown in Fig. 8 (a-c), under 365 nm UV irradiation, the blank nanofiber film has no fluorescence emission and afterglow emission. However, in Fig. 8 (d-f), the SSA nanofiber membrane exhibits strong blue fluorescence emission and green afterglow emission This indicates that the doping of SSA composites changes the original optical properties of the nanofiber membrane and gives it unique phosphorescent properties.

In conclusion, by incorporating SSA composites and combining with electrospinning technology, we have successfully prepared nanofiber membranes with unique phosphorescent properties. This innovation not only endows the fiber membrane with brand new functional properties, but also makes the hydrophobicity of the original fiber membrane significantly improved. Combining these two advantages offers new insights for exploring applications in anti-counterfeiting and encryption.

In this study, SSA composites were successfully prepared using a surface modification method. This method combines covalent modification with physical adsorption to ensure the stability of material modification. In this process, we introduced hydrophobic long carbon chains through the esterification of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which were tightly bound to the CDs@SiO2 composites using stable ester bonds. This imparts hydrophobicity to the SSA composites and enhances their stability in oil-soluble environments. In order to deeply explore the properties of SSA composites, we employed a variety of characterization tools. These studies not only revealed the structural morphology of SSA composites, but also demonstrated their unique optical properties and confirmed their stable oil solubility. In addition, this study has innovatively realized the combination of SSA composites with electrostatic spinning technology. Through this combination, we successfully developed a nanofiber membrane with anti-counterfeiting function. This fiber membrane not only possesses unique anti-counterfeiting properties, but its hydrophobicity is also significantly improved. This study provides new ideas and references for the modification strategy and application direction of inorganic room temperature phosphorescent materials.

Bingfu Lei: Writing – review & editing. Wei Li: Methodology. Meixue You: Software. Weihao Ye: Resources. Xinyu Huang: Methodology. Yan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yingliang Liu: Writing – review & editing. Xiaotang Liu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12174119).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134944.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong lifetime room-temperature phosphorescence in aqueous medium from silica confined polymer carbon dots for autoluminescence-free bioimaging and multilevel information encryption
Dyes Pigm.
